brand.name: Intel Halal
brand.tagline: Your Personal Muslim Companion in Japan

cekHalal.title: Halal Check
cekHalal.subtitle: Photo Japanese product packaging. AI reads ingredients and gives a halal, syubhat, or haram verdict.

onboarding.step1Desc: Camera, barcode, or photo from gallery.
onboarding.step2Desc: Make sure the ingredient list/原材料名 is clearly visible.
onboarding.step3Desc: System checks ingredients against the halal database.
onboarding.disclaimer: AI is indicative. For products with MUI/JAKIM labels, always prioritize official certification.
onboarding.kamusDesc: Learn common ingredient kanji without scanning first

verdict.label: VERDICT
verdict.disclaimer: AI Analysis · indicative · not a substitute for MUI / JAKIM certification

camera.errorCamera: Failed to access camera. Use Gallery instead.
camera.errorProcess: Failed to process. Please check your internet connection.

beaImpor.title: Import Tax
beaImpor.subtitle: Calculate IMEI phone tax for items bought in Japan before reaching Indonesian customs.

result.bebasPajakNote: Price below the $500 limit — all clear!

result.disclaimerFull: Estimate uses Ministry of Finance rate logic. Declare at Red Lane to get the $500 free limit.

tripPlan.title: Trip Plan
tripPlan.subtitle: AI creates a story-style guide for Japan's trains and buses — so you won't get lost on the Shinkansen.
tripPlan.loadingNote: Finding the best train and bus combination for you.
tripPlan.error: Failed to build route. Please check your internet connection.

findPlace.title: Find Place
findPlace.subtitle: Find mosques, halal restaurants, hotels, and important places near you.

location.errorGps: Failed to get location. Try turning off VPN.

empty.title: Not in dictionary yet
empty.desc: The vocabulary you searched for isn't here yet. Try scanning directly via Halal Check — AI can read any kanji on packaging.

categoryDesc.pork: Pork and its derivatives — always haram
categoryDesc.alcohol: Sake, mirin, and fermented alcohol derivatives
categoryDesc.plant: Soy, wheat, and other plant-based ingredients
categoryDesc.sauce: Shoyu, miso, dashi — check for alcohol/fish content

buta.arti: Pork. Its meat is haram according to Islamic law.
buta.tips: If kanji 豚 appears in ingredients or product name — skip immediately. Includes derivatives: 豚脂, 豚由来, 豚ゼラチン.

lard.arti: Lard — rendered pork fat. Often used as cooking oil and shortening.
lard.tips: Different from 牛脂 (beef fat) and 植物油脂 (vegetable oil). If unsure between ラード and ショートニング, treat as doubtful.

sake.arti: Sake — alcoholic beverage from fermented rice. Also appears as a cooking ingredient (料理酒).
sake.tips: Includes variants 清酒, 日本酒, 料理酒. If listed in food ingredients, consider haram even after cooking.

mirin.arti: Mirin — sweet condiment with 8–14% alcohol content. 本みりん (hon mirin) has the highest alcohol.
mirin.tips: みりん風調味料 (mirin-fū) is mirin-flavored with no alcohol — still check the label. 本みりん = definitely alcoholic.

nyukazai.arti: Emulsifier — binds oil and water. Can come from soy (halal) or animal fat (haram/doubtful).
nyukazai.tips: Look for source info: 乳化剤(大豆由来) means soy-derived → halal. No source listed = doubtful.

shortening.arti: Shortening — solid fat for pastry. Can be vegetable-based (palm/soy) or animal-based (lard).
shortening.tips: No 'vegetable' / 植物性 label → doubtful. If it says 植物油脂100%, usually safe.

kouryou.arti: Flavoring/fragrance. Can be plant-based, synthetic, or from animal extracts. Source is rarely disclosed.
kouryou.tips: If only written as "香料" without further info, treat as doubtful. Contact manufacturer if consumed regularly.

tori.arti: Chicken. Status depends on slaughter method — must be dzabihah (Islamic) to be halal.
tori.tips: Look for 'Halal Chicken' label or buy from Muslim stores. Japanese restaurants generally don't guarantee halal slaughter.

gyuu.arti: Beef. Same as chicken — depends on slaughter method. Regular supermarket beef in Japan is not halal.
gyuu.tips: Look for halal certification (印) or Australian/Brazilian halal supplier. Local Wagyu is generally non-halal.

sakana.arti: Fish. Most Islamic schools of thought consider all fish halal without requiring slaughter.
sakana.tips: Beware of accompanying sauces — often contain mirin or sake. Raw sashimi is safe, but the sauces need checking.

ebi.arti: Shrimp. Halal according to the majority of schools (Shafi'i, Maliki, Hanbali). Hanafi has a different view.
ebi.tips: Tempura batter sometimes uses egg & dashi — still check ingredients. Dipping sauces usually contain mirin.

daizu.arti: Soybean. Base ingredient for tofu, miso, shoyu, natto, and many vegetable emulsifiers.
daizu.tips: "大豆由来" on emulsifier label = green signal. Plain tofu & soymilk are almost always safe.

komugi.arti: Wheat. Base for noodles, bread, tempura flour, and most Japanese pastries.
komugi.tips: Flour itself is halal, but processed products are often mixed with dashi/fat. Check dry noodle & commercial bread ingredients.

satou.arti: Sugar. White, brown (黒糖), or powdered — all halal as standalone ingredients.
satou.tips: Safe on its own, but sugar used in processed products doesn't guarantee the final product is halal.

hachimitsu.arti: Honey. Halal and recommended in Islam.
hachimitsu.tips: Sometimes mixed with royal jelly or other extracts — check label if not 100% pure honey.

shouyu.arti: Shoyu/Japanese soy sauce. Fermentation produces alcohol — final content 1–3%.
shouyu.tips: Look for halal-certified shoyu (Kikkoman Halal, etc.) or labeled "アルコール無添加". Standard version = doubtful.

miso.arti: Fermented soybean paste. Similar to shoyu — fermentation produces a small amount of alcohol.
miso.tips: Look for halal-certified miso. Alcohol content is usually <1% but still doubtful without certification.

dashi.arti: Japanese broth from fish (かつお), seaweed (昆布), or sardine (煮干し). Sometimes mixed with sake/mirin.
dashi.tips: Pure kombu dashi = halal. Commercial dashi often adds chicken/pork extract & mirin — check 原材料名.
